content: The press section follows the forming section on a paper machine. Its functions include continued water removal, sheet consolidation, sheet support, sheet transfer and surface texture development.
The terms press fabric and press felt are commonly used interchangeably in the paper industry although technically a felt refers to a fabric made only from fibers without woven yarns.
Typical sheet consistency entering the press section is about 20 percent fiber and 80 percent water, while exiting consistency is approximately 40 percent fiber and 60 percent water.
Water removal in the press section occurs when the sheet is compressed between rolls and press fabrics in the nip, forcing water out of the sheet structure.
Main functions of a press fabric include water removal, sheet support, sheet transportation, pressure distribution and imparting proper surface finish to the paper.
Additional functions of press fabrics include transferring the sheet in closed draws and driving undriven felt rolls and cylinder formers.
Void volume is the volume within the press fabric not occupied by fibers or yarns and determines the amount of water the fabric can absorb.
Important press fabric properties include strength, void volume, permeability, low compressibility, batt-to-base ratio, abrasion resistance, contaminant resistance and chemical resistance.
Flow-controlled nips are characterized by high water loads, heavyweight sheets, slow drainage and low freeness stock where water flow resistance in the sheet controls dewatering.
Major problems in flow-controlled nips include sheet crushing and hydraulic flow marking.
Water removal in flow-controlled nips is aided by soft roll covers, large diameter rolls, double felting and high sheet temperatures.
Flow-controlled nips require low flow resistance, high permeability and high void volume structures, often achieved with multilayer or laminated fabrics.
Pressure-controlled nips are characterized by low water loads, lightweight sheets, fast drainage and high freeness stock where mechanical compression resistance controls dewatering.
Major symptoms of pressure-controlled nip problems include vibration, poor sheet consistency and impression marking.
Water removal in pressure-controlled nips is aided by hard roll covers, small diameter rolls, pressing uniformity and high sheet temperatures.
Pressure-controlled nips require high pressure uniformity, good mass uniformity and finer batt deniers to improve pressure distribution.
Transversal flow pressing consists of several phases involving sheet saturation, hydraulic pressure generation, maximum dryness and rewetting during expansion after the nip.
Rewetting occurs in the outgoing nip when the sheet and press fabric expand and water returns from the fabric to the paper due to capillary forces and pressure differences.
An ideal press fabric should provide uniform pressure distribution, low flow resistance and minimum rewetting.
Important variables influencing press fabric design include water load, nip pressure, nip width, speed, dwell time, sheet properties and temperature.
First presses with high water loads behave more like flow-controlled nips, while later presses with lower water loads behave more like pressure-controlled nips.
Modern press fabrics evolved from woven wool felts to synthetic needled structures with improved strength, drainage and durability.
Needling is the process of mechanically locking batt fibers into the base fabric using barbed needles.
Press fabrics generally consist of two main components: a woven base fabric and batt fibers attached by needling.
Most modern press fabric base structures are manufactured from synthetic materials, primarily polyamide or nylon polymers.
Common yarn types used in press fabric base structures include cabled monofilament, multifilament, spun yarn and single monofilament.
Monofilament yarns are durable, resistant to compression and have better chemical resistance than multifilament yarns.
Multifilament yarns are more supple and compressible than monofilament yarns but have lower resistance to chemical attack.
Seam fabrics improve installation safety and reduce installation time. Main seam types include pin seam and woven loop seam designs.
Batt denier selection depends on water handling requirements, break-in time, vacuum capacity, sheet control, filling tendency and bleed-through tendency.
